title: Estimating Career Benefits from Online Community Leadership: Evidence from Stack Exchange Moderators
abstract: Many IT professionals seek to improve their job prospects by engaging as leaders of online communities, for example, by serving as a moderator or admin. We investigate whether such community leadership leads to (causal) improvements in individuals’ careers. We assemble a data set, including job histories of IT professionals who have sought election as moderators (mods) in Stack Exchange question-and-answer communities, between 2010 and 2020. We estimate the career benefits of moderatorship under two complementary identification strategies: difference-in-differences (DID) and regression discontinuity (RD). We observe qualitatively consistent results under each design, finding that election to a moderator role has a significant, causal, positive effect on job mobility. We estimate that moderatorship increases the probability of a job change by between 4.7 and 12.3 percentage points over the two years following the election. We also report a series of secondary analyses that speak to associated salary increases and show evidence consistent with the notion that social capital and signaling play a role. Our findings help us understand the benefits of online community leadership, and they extend our understanding of the motivations for online community engagement.

author.Jens Forderer: TUM School of Management, Campus Heilbronn, Technical University of Munich, 74076 Heilbronn, Germany
author.Gordon Burtch: Questrom School of Business, Boston University, Boston, Massachusetts 02215

article.1. Introduction: A growing body of evidence indicates that IT professionals’ contributions toward online communities are motivated by career concerns (Lerner and Tirole 2002,Wasko and Faraj 2005,Roberts et al. 2006,Xu et al. 2020).1Among such contributions, perhaps the most significant are those made by individuals who take on a formal leadership role, for example, serving as community moderators or managing open-source software projects (Faraj et al. 2015,Johnson et al. 2015,Safadi et al. 2021). Serving in a leadership capacity can require several hours of work per day, and the work is typically not compensated, implying opportunity costs. Despite the claim that community contributions can provide career benefits, little evidence exists to support it, a gap we seek to address here. Identifying the career benefits of community leadership is difficult because detailed data on individuals’ careers are generally difficult to obtain. Community leaders seldom share information publicly with the community about their backgrounds or personal lives. When such information is disclosed, it tends to reflect points in time prior to—rather than following—their tenure in a community leadership position, because individuals often disclose such information when seeking to build a rapport with the community they hope to lead. Even if full job trajectories were available, it is difficult to determine what portion of career advancements truly derived from individuals’ assumption of community leadership roles. The decision to engage in a community is endogenous, and thus a comparison of leaders’ career trajectories against those of non-leaders would very likely embed noncausal sources of association; that is, differences between leaders and non-leaders would reflect confounding factors that simultaneously drive both community engagement and career progression (e.g., talent, ability, grit). It is therefore not surprising that evidence on the career benefits of community leadership is scarce and, at best, correlational. We seek to overcome these challenges via a novel integration of alternative data sources and complementary empirical strategies. We study Stack Exchange, a platform that hosts question-and-answer communities on a variety of subjects (e.g., software development, sciences, or law). Each community is led by a few individuals, so-called moderators. A moderator’s job involves adding or removing members, reviewing and rejecting posts, and resolving conflicts. Importantly, moderators are democratically elected by each community, which allows us to assess the job outcomes for any given candidate as a function of whether they won (versus lost) an election. Our sample is thus comprised of candidates who ran for election in several Stack Exchange communities between 2010 (when elections first began) and 2020. We manually identified candidates’ LinkedIn profiles and collected their posted job histories. To operationalize career benefits, we focus on the occurrence of job changes. Job changes are a meaningful indicator of career benefits because job changes are most often associated with career advancement (Topel and Ward 1992,Zippia 2023), and most job changes are voluntary in nature, implying that they are more likely to result from worker preferences than layoffs or firings (BLS 2018).2 We rely on two complementary empirical strategies to identify causal estimates. Our first strategy is a difference-in-differences (DID) design with matching, wherein we compare the job changes before and after elections, contrasting those outcomes between moderatorship candidates successfully elected and similar yet nonetheless unelected candidates. This DID yields a global average treatment effect (ATE) but relies on a strong set of assumptions regarding omitted variable bias. Our second identification strategy is thus a regression-discontinuity design (RDD). The RDD requires relatively weaker assumptions to establish causality (Imbens and Lemieux 2008) but restricts the candidate field to a smaller sample and yields only a local average treatment effect (LATE). We embed both estimations within a hazard modeling framework, consistent with other recent work studying factors that influence career shifts (see, e.g.,Card et al. 2007,Chetty 2008, andYang 2017). We observe consistent results under both estimation strategies; we find that taking on a moderator role within a Stack Exchange community has a significant, causal, positive effect on the probability that an individual experiences a purposeful job change. In particular, the probability of a job change increases by between 4.7 and 12.3 percentage points. This study makes three contributions to the literature. First, our work contributes to the debate on the connection between career benefits and engagement in online communities (Roberts et al. 2006,Hann et al. 2013,Xu et al. 2020). Our study provides, what is to our knowledge, the first causal evidence that community engagement yields concrete career benefits. Our study is thus in line with prior research, which has suggested that community contributions may have value as a quality signal in the job market (Hann et al. 2013,Huang and Zhang 2016). Second, we extend past work by exploring specific mechanisms behind the observed effects, finding suggestive evidence for a social capital mechanism in particular. Third, we contribute to the literature on individual motivations for contributing to online knowledge communities (see, e.g.,Lakhani and Von Hippel 2003,Wasko and Faraj 2005, andSmirnova et al. 2022). Our study suggests that the perceived economic benefits of community engagement, both in general and specifically in terms of moderatorship, are well-founded. Extrinsic, economic incentives are not merely perceived; they are a reality. This has implications for platform operators because economic and social incentives have been shown to have differential impacts on the frequency and quality of user-generated content (Burtch et al. 2018,Kummer et al. 2020). Our work presents an empirical strategy for studying these relationships in other communities and for exploring contingencies of where, how, and for whom career benefits manifest.
article.2. Theoretical Foundation: 2.1. Motivations to Contribute to Online Communities Knowledge production is increasingly performed by volunteers in online communities, such as those behind question-and-answer forums (Goes et al. 2016,Xu et al. 2020), online encyclopedias (Aaltonen and Seiler 2016,Gallus 2017,Loh and Kretschmer 2023), and open-source software (von Hippel and von Krogh 2003,Maruping et al. 2019). We define online communities (hereafter, communities) as collectives in which volunteers with shared interests participate and interact with the goal of creating an overall outcome mediated by information and communication technology (Armstrong and Hagel 2000). Extant research focuses on understanding the drivers of contributions to online communities, which is important because an understanding of contributor motivations helps inform the design and management of communities, for example, in terms of incentive mechanisms (Goes et al. 2016,Kokkodis et al. 2020). Previous research documented various drivers, from mere opportunity (Kummer et al. 2020), to different motivations, such as hobbyism (Jeppesen and Frederiksen 2006), the satisfaction of needs (Lakhani and Von Hippel 2003), monetary compensation (Wang et al. 2022), hierarchy (Pu et al. 2022), and peer recognition (Wasko and Faraj 2005,Gallus 2017,Burtch et al. 2022,Smirnova et al. 2022). These results mirror the labor economics literature, which has considered the motivations for volunteering (see, e.g.,Freeman 1997). Various motives have been observed, including warm-glow altruism (Andreoni 1990,Kotchen and Wagner 2023), charitable giving (Duncan 1999), and tax incentives (Duquette 2016). However, that literature also notes that volunteers may view their contributions as an investment in their future career if the work allows one to acquire new skills, make valuable new contacts, or gain experience (see, e.g.,Menchik and Weisbrod 1987). There are also several relevant studies dealing with the economics of public goods and individual contributions toward public goods. A broad question in that literature pertains to individuals’ choice between free-riding and voluntary contributions (see, e.g.,Andreoni 1990andBénabou and Tirole 2006). In the specific context of online voluntary work, the focus has been on understanding antecedents to contributions to inform the design of platform incentives and interventions (see, e.g.,Chen et al. 2010andZhang and Zhu 2011). For example,Chen et al. (2010)andBurtch et al. (2018)designed and evaluated experimental interventions that play on social norms. 2.2. Community Leadership We follow existing research and define a community leader as a member possessing formal authority over a community, in terms of acting in a leader role (Faraj et al. 2015,Johnson et al. 2015,Safadi et al. 2021). The tasks of a leader are to define community-level parameters (e.g., project goal, scope) and to ensure a high quality of community participation. There are different practices by which communities select their leaders, including formal elections, appointment by a council, or more informal approaches, such aspassing the baton(Burke and Kraut 2008). Several studies have investigated community leadership (Faraj et al. 2015,Johnson et al. 2015). Their focus is to characterize the traits of users who are perceived as leaders by the community, yet much less attention has been paid to those individuals who hold a formal leadership role.Johnson et al. (2015)andFaraj et al. (2015), for example, explored the behavioral and structural antecedents of leadership in online communities, suggesting that leaders show greater contributions, power, and sociability. Other work has sought to identify predictors of formal leadership;Burke and Kraut (2008)identified factors that influence the likelihood of becoming a Wikipedia admin, andShah (2006)provided insights on the mechanisms that communities employ to determine their leaders. Yet we still lack a clear understanding of the career benefits one may derive from taking on such a position. 2.3. Career Benefits from Community Engagement Of relevance to the present study is the role that economic motives may play in driving a desire to lead.Lerner and Tirole (2002)argued that participating in open-source communities may operate as a springboard for advancing in the corporate world, and they provided some anecdotal evidence. Subsequent studies have documented career benefits as a motivation for community engagement. For example,Roberts et al. (2006)found that paid open-source contributors exhibit higher levels of participation.Xu et al. (2020)observed that Stack Overflow users reduce their intensity of contribution immediately following a job change. AndBitzer and Geishecker (2010)found that university dropouts show a higher probability of contributing to open-source projects, perhaps to improve their career prospects.Kummer et al. (2020)andMalgonde et al. (2023)documented a positive relationship between unemployment and online community contributions. An open question, however, is the extent to which community contributions truly translate into career benefits. At least two studies have provided evidence in this regard.Huang and Zhang (2016)demonstrated that active participation in SAP’s online community correlates positively with the likelihood of job-hopping.Hann et al. (2013)found that holding a higher rank in an open-source community correlates positively with wages. In the wider literature on volunteer work in labor economics, whether and to what extent volunteer work provides career benefits likewise remains an open question. Volunteer work could be perceived negatively by employers, were they to conclude that the volunteering diverts an individual’s attention from their career or profession. However, recent evidence suggests some positive career benefit from volunteering (Scharf and Smith 2016,Baert and Vujić 2018,Piopiunik et al. 2020). For example,Piopiunik et al. (2020)found that graduates were more likely to receive a job interview invitation if they had volunteering experience. 2.4. Theoretical Mechanisms Three theories provide alternative—albeit not mutually exclusive—explanations for how career benefits might manifest: human capital, social capital, and signaling theory. Human capital theoryargues that a worker’s marketable skills are capital and that the worker can increase this capital by making investments in skills (Becker 1962). Human capital includes the skills, competencies, and knowledge that individuals can use to increase their productivity. It can explain substantial variation in wage growth (Mithas and Krishnan 2008,Lazear 2009). This capital can take the form of knowledge or any other factor that directly contributes to a worker’s productivity. Employment benefits from community participation may therefore derive from gains in human capital. Community participation may create human capital by developing an individual’s technical skills, especially to keep up with technological advances (Zaza et al. 2023). For instance, a user may learn as a result of reviewing computer code or gain an improved understanding from explaining a complex technical concept to others or from engaging in leadership (Lerner and Tirole 2002). Social capital theoryprovides an alternative theoretical rationale (Adler and Kwon 2002,Coleman 1994). Social capital refers to “the sum of actual and potential resources embedded within, available through, and derived from, the network of relationships possessed by that individual” (Nahapiet and Ghoshal 1998, p. 243). Together with the human capital view, the social capital view advocates the notion that an individual’s resources are the foundation of career benefits. Individuals with greater social capital may draw on their connections to advance their career. Community engagement is likely to yield social capital because interactions within the community are broadly social and often interpersonal because leaders seldom remain anonymous (Butler et al. 2007,Johnson et al. 2015). These interactions can thus lead to new, meaningful interpersonal relationships, which in turn can advance a career. Signaling theoryis based on the argument that firms have imperfect information about worker productivity, and individuals invest in credentials to credibly convey information about their abilities (Arrow 1973,Spence 1978). Signaling theory argues that employers cannot directly observe worker quality and that a worker’s true quality is only known to him- or herself, that is, the presence of information asymmetries. The informational value of a credential (e.g., a diploma or a degree) comes from employers’ belief that the credential is costly to acquire and positively correlated with a candidate’s true abilities. Community leadership may represent such a signal because firms may infer that an individual holds genuine interest, experience, and endurance.
article.3. Context and Data: 3.1. Study Context We examine the causal effect of community leadership on career benefits in the context of Stack Exchange, a network of more than 160 online question-and-answer communities. At the time of the study, Stack Exchange collectively hosted more than 20 million questions, with associated answers, contributed by more than 13 million members. Most Stack Exchange communities address IT-related topics (e.g., databases, Unix), but there are also communities focused on non-IT domains (e.g., natural sciences, languages). Each community usually has a group of between two and 30 leaders, so-called moderators (mods). Mods are expected to contribute expert knowledge and to lead by example (Stack Exchange 2009). Moderators add or remove community members, review or reject postings, and resolve conflicts. Moderators tend to have an overall greater level of exposure to community members because of their role, and social interactions are a substantial part of their day-to-day activities. Moderatorship may also provide signaling benefits, because an employer may infer that moderators have both knowledge and expertise in their community’s subject matter area. Furthermore, moderatorship can be perceived as useful leadership experience because it bears formal, codified leadership and administrative responsibilities. Stack Exchange mods are selected through democratic, closed-ballot elections held separately in each community, approximately once a year. Typically, multiple moderator seats are filled in tandem via vote. An election encompasses nomination, primary, and election phases. In the nomination phase, usually seven days long, members can suggest candidates as well as self-nominate. Candidates write a motivation statement and defend themselves in a public Q & A. For example, candidates may be asked about their schedule availability and prior experience. Candidates may also be challenged by members of the community. During the four-day primary, voters can allocate one up- or down-vote per candidate. The top 10 candidates by vote score move to the final election. During the four-day final election, voting follows a single transferrable vote (STV) system, described in Online Appendix B. The elections rely on a secret ballot, the display of the candidates on the ballots is randomized, and safeguards are in place to prevent manipulation. Moderatorship is granted for an unlimited period. Moderator status is indicated by a diamond symbol displayed next to the username. 3.2. Data We obtain a list of all users that have run for a position as a moderator (candidates) in a Stack Exchange community between December 2010, when the first elections were held, and March 2020. We collected data on candidates, seats, votes, and the outcomes from each community’s election web page.3The initial sample included 1,776 unique candidates across 113 communities and 281 elections. Candidates can run for moderatorship repeatedly, and we keep these repeated candidacies in our sample. To obtain job histories, we manually searched for each candidate’s profile on the professional networking platform LinkedIn. Many candidates disclose their profiles during the nomination phase, along with other personal details. Accordingly, we were able to identify LinkedIn profiles for 65% of all candidates. We retained those candidates who could broadly be considered IT professionals, namely those who held job roles such as software developers, software architects, and data scientists, or who held degrees with either a major or a minor in computer science. This reduced the sample to 937 candidates. Further details are available in the supplementary files. 3.3. Measures 3.3.1. Career Benefits. We infer career benefits based primarily on the occurrence of job changes. We argue that job changes are a meaningful measure of career benefits for at least three reasons. First, prior research, surveys, and industry reports indicate that job changes among tech workers tend to be career improvements. For example,Topel and Ward (1992)reported that job changes increase worker wages by an estimated 10% on average. Recent surveys and industry reports tell a similar story. Survey results from Pew indicate that most workers who experienced job changes in the last few years experienced wage gains (Pew Research Center 2022). Industry reports indicate that job hopping in the information sector tends to be driven by increased compensation (LinkedIn 2018) and that these job changes result in an approximately 9.7% increase in a worker’s wages, a wage benefit larger than that observed in many other industries (Zippia 2023). Second, most job changes are voluntary. Historically, the ratio ofquitsto involuntary separations (layoffs and dismissals) in the United States, otherwise known as the Q/LD ratio, has been greater than 1. This implies that, in general, workers are more likely to leave a job voluntarily than to be fired or laid off (BLS 2018). This is particularly true in the information sector, which frequently exhibited a higher Q/LD ratio than other industries over the period spanning 2000 through 2017. Thus, job changes in the tech sector are very much driven by worker preferences, not firings (BLS 2018). Third, our focus on job changes has the benefit of making our study more directly comparable to prior research (Huang and Zhang 2016,Xu et al. 2020). For example,Huang and Zhang (2016)studied contributions to an online community and similarly relied on job changes as a measure of career improvement. The above points aside, we also validate our assumption that positive effects on job changes amount to career benefits by considering salary data as an alternative measure, as we describe below. To identify job changes, we extract candidates’ job history from their LinkedIn profile and code breakpoints (Job change). Here, we include job changes within the same company, and we also include job changes with intermediary employment gaps. We also consider an alternative measure, namely an estimate of a candidate’s job salary (Salary). To construct this measure, we first standardize the job roles reported in candidates’ job histories (e.g.,Senior SDK DevelopertoSenior Developer) and then obtain the average annual salary for that job role within the employing company from GlassDoor, an online platform where employees share salary information. Whenever company-role-specific data were not available, we imputed it using data on the same job role at the smallest available geographical area (i.e., city or region, country). Because not all candidates reported their employing company or region, salary data are available only for a subset of individuals. 3.3.2. Treatment. Modis 1 if candidateiwas elected as a moderator. 3.3.3. Education and Career-Related. Based on the LinkedIn profiles, we create indicator variables that capture different levels of educational attainment of a candidate, that is, undergraduate degree and doctoral degree. We also create career-related variables.Workxp (yrs)isi’s total number of years in employment, and we round the variable to 1 when a candidate has less than one year of work experience.Workxp (jbs)isi’s total number of jobs. Both variables are measured for the pre-election period. 3.3.4. Participation. We also create variables capturing candidatei’s participation in the community.Answersis the number of answers contributed byi;Questionscaptures the number of questions submitted byi;Editsindicates the number of posts by other users that were edited byi;Badgescaptures the count of badges (i.e., awards for participation) thatihas received;Commentsis the number of comments posted byi; andSuggestionscaptures the count of suggestions for edits of others’ posts thatihas made. These variables—unless noted otherwise—are observed over the half-year before a candidate’s election date for the purpose of assessing pre-election heterogeneity. In the few cases where a candidate runs multiple times in an election, the variable holds the data for the respective half-year before the current election. Each of these variables is logged to address right skewness. 3.3.5. Mechanisms. We proxy for human capital acquisition following an election by considering a candidate’s subsequent accumulation of knowledge tags in the community. Stack Exchange relies on a system of tags to categorize questions (e.g.,Python,Android). The more tags a candidate is involved in, the more human capital accumulation we might infer. Tag badges are automatically conferred to users if their activity exceeds a certain threshold of activity (e.g., providing answers or edits) around posts on a specific tag.Tag badgesis the count of tag badges acquired by candidateiover the second half-year following the election (i.e.,t+ 1).4Note that acquisition of these badges plausibly reflects only human capital accumulation; these badges are not likely to provide any signaling benefits because, unlike the receipt of a diploma or degree, the effort and skill involved in acquiring these badges is nonstandard and hard for an employer to infer, given that the badge names are unintuitive for employers (e.g., “Necromancer”, “Lifejacket”). We infer social capital from the number of individuals a candidate has interacted with in the Stack Exchange chat. Chats hint at interactions between users and provide an understanding of the social capital of a user. Stack Exchange relies on a public chat room system. Whereas users can create private rooms (i.e., rooms that are accessible by invitation only), all other messages are publicly available along with timestamps. We create the variableChat contactsas the number of users who had sent a direct message to candidatei; consistent to the human capital measure, we capture the activity over the second half-year following the election (i.e.,t+ 1). We count only incoming messages, because outbound messages may reflect mere interest in forming connections, rather than actual reciprocal communication.5We log transform both measures. 3.3.6. Community and Election. Stack Exchange reports community and election data on the election website of each community.Community sizeis equal to the number of unique, eligible voters who visited the forum between the beginning of the nominations and the end of the election. A unique visit refers to the fact that a voter opened the forum website, regardless of how often they visited. We log-transform the measure. We defineElection participationas the number of unique, eligible voters who visited the forum during the election phase (i.e.,Community size), divided by the total number of eligible voters as reported on the election webpage of each community. Higher participation in the election may lead to increased awareness and transparency, which in turn may influence election outcomes. Data on the number of eligible voters is reported on the Stack Exchange election pages.Moderatorsis the ratio of the number of moderator positions toCommunity size. This ratio may hint at the moderators’ workload. We create dummies for the community subject-matter theme, as derived from the official categorization provided by Stack Exchange at the time of the study, namely business (e.g., Project Management), culture (e.g., German language), life (e.g., Gardening & Landscaping), professional (e.g., Freelancing), science (e.g., Biology), and technology (e.g., Stack Overflow). We also add several variables that the communities display to voters aboutias information about the merits of a candidate.Reputationholds the reputation score ofiat the time of the nomination. In addition, the candidate display includes information about the achievements of certain badges. Therefore, complementary to the generalBadgesvariable, we capture these specific badge types in separate variables.Editing badgesis the count of badges received byifor editing activity (i.e., given to honor contributions with regard to editing posts).Moderation badgesis the count of badges for moderation activity received byi(i.e., given to honor contributions that help the actual moderators, such as flagging posts).Participation badgesis the count of badges for participation activity received byi(e.g., badges given for completing the user profile description or regular visits of the community). To adjust for community-specific differences in the reputation distribution as well as the requirements to obtain badges, we normalize these variables byCommunity size, and we log-transform them to account for right skewness. article.4. Estimation and Identification Strateg: 4.1. Hazard Framework Following research on labor mobility and to account for the fact that job change events are distributed in time and are right censored for all candidates who do not change employers by the end of the observation window, we employ a hazard model (see, e.g.,Card et al. 2007,Chetty 2008,Yang 2017).6Hazard models are preferable when the focus is on understanding the relationship between theriskof an event occurring at timetand the explanatory variables of interest at the same point in time (Lancaster 1990). We employ a Cox proportional hazard (PH) model because, unlike other hazard approaches, it does not impose any assumptions on the functional form of the baseline hazard rate (Cox 1972,Lancaster 1990). 4.2. Identification Strategy We employ two distinct yet complementary identification strategies: a difference-in-differences design (DID) with matching and a regression discontinuity design (RDD). The DID design compares the occurrence of job changes before versus after elections, contrasting elected and unelected candidates. The DID has the dual benefit of yielding an ATE and helping to account for unobserved time-invariant heterogeneity between candidates via the incorporation of candidate fixed effects in addition to matching. The (sharp) RDD is a quasi-experimental design that allows the identification of a LATE when treatment assignment changes discontinuously at a threshold. Following the ample literature on the use of RDD in studying electoral races, we compare the job outcomes of candidates within a neighborhood (bandwidth) around the winning threshold (Imbens and Lemieux 2008,Lee and Lemieux 2010,Eggers et al. 2015). The running (forcing) variable in this setup is the candidate’s vote share margin, and the cutoff is the vote threshold at which a candidate is elected. Compared with a DID design, an RDD rests on fewer assumptions when it comes to identification, because it relies to a lesser degree on the direct observation of all relevant covariates (Angrist and Pischke 2009). The downside of the RDD is that it yields only a LATE, and furthermore, it focuses on a smaller, pruned sample (Imbens and Lemieux 2008). 4.3. Difference-in-Differences Design 4.3.1. Panel Construction and Matching Techniques. LikeChetty (2008)andMarinescu (2009), we construct our data set for the DID estimation as an employment spell panel. We consider a pre-election and post-election period of two years around each election, to ensure that we observe a lengthy period of pre-election activity, while also ensuring a reasonably large estimation sample. This means that we split the employment history of a candidate into spells that begin with a job change and end with the subsequent job change. We then transform the spells with the remainder of the data into a half-year panel such that a spell can begin or end with only a half-year. We follow the approach ofCengiz et al. (2019), aligning elections in relative time.7To reduce heterogeneity, we employ propensity score matching (PSM) and coarsened exact matching (CEM). Regarding PSM, we employ logistic nearest-neighbor matching (Rosenbaum and Rubin 1983). Regarding CEM, we define the cut points manually to obtain balance in post-matching tests of mean differences (Iacus et al. 2011). For both procedures, we match on the following variables; for the time-variant variables based on the half-year preceding an election: Log(Answers), Log(Questions), Log(Edits), Log(Badges), Log(Comments), Log(Suggestions), Log(Reputation), Log(Editing badges), Log(Moderation badges), Log(Participation badges), Log(Tag badges), and Log(Chat contacts).8Matching on these variables ensures the comparability of candidates in terms of their community contributions and the performance criteria that are publicly displayed about candidates during the election. We do not match on pretreatmentJob change, because recent work notes that this can introduce bias into the estimation (Ding and Li 2019). This procedure yields three panels, one based on CEM (hereafter DID-CEM), one based on PSM (hereafter DID-PSM), and one that includes all candidates, that is, without matching (hereafter DID-All). FollowingChetty (2008)andMarinescu (2009), the DID then takes the form 4.4. Regression Discontinuity Design 4.4.1. Majority Threshold and Sample Construction. The sample construction for the RDD depends on the election system. Stack Exchange elections rely on a preference-based, single-transferrable vote system (STV). In an STV system, voters name and rank their top three candidates on a ballot. Votes are then iteratively reallocated based on voters’ candidate rankings. In each round, a quota is fixed. If a candidate’s votes reach that quota, then that candidate is elected. Any votes exceeding the quota are redistributed based on the second and third preferences on the ballot. If none of the remaining candidates surpass the current quota in a given iteration, then the lowest-performing candidate is eliminated, and the excess votes for the candidate are again redistributed. Most elections involve several rounds of vote reallocation. Given this setup, in STV systems, elections are not won on the basis of a simple majority of initial votes (i.e., 50%); instead, each iteration produces a distinct election threshold (quota). To adjust the sharp RDD setup to the STV system, we follow the approach ofRedmond and Regan (2015); to obtain a meaningful majority cutoff, the sample is restricted to the pair of candidates who contested the final available seat in an election and after all vote transfers were made. For each final seat, one candidate will ultimately win (least-voted winner), and one will be the runner-up (most-voted loser). If candidatesiandjcontest the final seat, then the candidate who receives more than 50% of the remaining votes will become a moderator. Formally, if candidatesiandjcontest the final seat, then the equivalent vote share foriis thus calculated as the votes foridivided by the sum of votes foriandj. We exploit the sharp discontinuity at the 50% threshold to estimate the change in the dependent variable with assignment toMod. LetYidenote the dependent variable, and then we are interested in the discontinuity in the conditional expectation ofYigiven the vote share, to identify the causal effect,c, at the discontinuity. Candidateiis elected (Mod) ifi’s vote share is above 50% in the final round of vote reallocation. WithShare margin, we accordingly denote the vote share of candidateiminus the winning threshold of 50%. This RDD is based on a cross-sectional sample of candidates (instead of a candidate panel). That is, our estimation sample is comprised of one observation per candidate, and the outcome of interest is the duration of time to their next observed job change, with right censoring at the end of our observation window for candidates where no job change occurs. For a few elections, we observe only one final seat candidate (i.e., either the least-voted winner or the most-voted loser). We retain those elections in the sample because the RDD does only require probabilistic equivalence of the candidates. Moreover, as we document in the robustness section, there is no evidence of a systematic bias in data availability depending on the election outcome. 4.4.2. Regression Specification. We follow extant studies that used RDDs under hazard modeling frameworks (see, e.g.,Berk and de Leeuw 1999andCard et al. 2007) and define the model as 4.4.3. Bandwidth Selection and Comparability. The RDD is ultimately estimated on a restricted subset of candidates that lie within the neighborhood of the vote threshold, defined in terms of a bandwidth parameter. To select the bandwidth, we rely on the bandwidth selection algorithm ofImbens and Kalyanaraman (2012). The algorithm is based on minimizing an expected squared error loss criterion, which incorporates several factors, including the variance in outcomes at the cutoff, the density of the running variable, covariates, and the shapes of the curves on both sides. The idea of the criterion is that a larger bandwidth should be chosen if the variance in outcomes increases at the cutoff, when the density of the running variable is small, and the shapes of the curves at either side of the cutoff become symmetrical. The obtained bandwidth—in the following referred to asIK-bandwidth—is 15.9%. Recognizing that the IK-bandwidth in our case may be perceived as large, we report the results also for a bandwidth of 5%. It yields a large-enough sample to permit statistical tests but is considerably smaller than the IK-bandwidth. This procedure results in three samples, one containing all final seat candidates (hereafter, RDD-None), one containing final seat candidates within the IK-bandwidth (hereafter RDD-IK), and one containing final seat candidates within the 5% bandwidth (hereafter RDD-5%). None of these samples shows a statistically significant difference in job changes. Whereas we observe imbalance along some variables in RDD-None, we observe no significant imbalance in RDD-IK. Column “Difference” is given in percentage points and computed based on the exact (unrounded) probabilities for winners and nonwinners. There are only a few estimates available for comparison. Notably,Huang and Zhang (2016)found that a 1% increase in knowledge contributions (measured by the total helpful answers posted by users in an enterprise software community) is associated with a 1.2 percentage point increase in the probability of a job change. Our estimate is higher, indicating that leadership is associated with significantly greater career benefits than knowledge contributions. However, it is important to note that these estimates are not perfectly comparable. Two patterns in the estimates are important to be discussed.10The first pattern is that the effect magnitude for the smallest bandwidth (i.e., RDD-5%) is larger than for RDD-IK and RDD-None. A similar pattern is also evident in other RDD studies (see, e.g.,Ludwig and Miller 2007,Flammer 2015). One explanation is that the number of observations decreases with smaller bandwidths, thus making sampling variability a more important part of the estimate (seeLee and Lemieux 2010andCalonico et al. 2014). Indeed, this issue motivates using theImbens and Kalyanaraman (2012)bandwidth because it considers the variance in the outcome at the cutoff. Another explanation is also possible, namely that the baseline hazards in the respective subsamples differ. The second pattern is that the smallest effect magnitude is the one obtained from DID-All. One interpretation is that the endogeneity—which enters the estimation as we move from DID-All to the matched samples (i.e., DID-CEM and DID-PSM) or the discontinuity samples (i.e., RDD-IK, RDD-5%, RDD-None)—exerts a downward bias. As a result, the effect on the outcome variable may be underestimated in this model, leading to a smaller coefficient estimate. Arguably, among the estimated models, DID-All is one most prone to endogeneity because it is not adjusted to the pre-existing heterogeneity between winners and nonwinners documented inTable 2and because it does not account for unobserved variables negatively correlated with winning. Another interpretation is that DID-All provides an estimate of the ATE, whereas the matched and RDD estimates provide a LATE. The matching and RDD setups could shift the observed job changes to a subgroup of individuals for whom the career benefits from moderatorship are larger than average. InTable 2, the group means indicate that the matching and RDD setups tend to discard candidates from the sample who participated most actively before the election. For example, highly participating candidates could experience relatively smaller treatment effects, perhaps because they have already accumulated considerably human and social capital or because they possess signals other than moderatorship. 5.5. Further Evidence: Salary Estimation To bolster the interpretation of job changes as career benefits, we next consider wage effects.Tables 7and8report the results, which support a career benefit interpretation. For the DID, we estimate an increase by between 1.9% and 3.8%, although the estimates are statistically significant only in the case of the DID-All sample. For the RDD, the effects range from 17.7% to 24.7% and are statistically significant for the RDD-IK and RDD-None samples. article.6. Robustness: 6.1. Sensitivity The Online Appendix reports several sensitivity tests, side-by-side, for both the DID and the RDD. The results for job changes are consistent when using a Logit estimator (Online Appendix Tables A.4 and A.5). The RDD results for job changes and salary are consistent, using pooled, quadratic, and nonparametric specifications (Online Appendix Tables A.6–A.9). Regarding job changes after elections, 8.39% are within-employer and 0.19% reflect employment by Stack Overflow, Inc. Therefore, we also compare the estimates when omitting job changes from the measure that involve Stack Overflow, Inc. as the employer, the company operating the communities, or omitting company-internal job changes (i.e., by recoding them as no job change; Online Appendix Table A.10). Finally, we test the sensitivity toward the exclusion of subsamples. First, we explore dropping candidates from the Stack Overflow community, the largest community on Stack Exchange. Dropping candidates reduces the sample size and therefore statistical power; thus, to enable a careful interpretation, we proceed stepwise, dropping 10% of the candidates at random and reporting the estimate, then 20%, and so on. Overall, the results are robust to removing these candidates, with the exception that for DID-All the effects fall below the significance level when we omit 80% and more of the candidates (Online Appendix Figure A.1). Second, we explore dropping repeat candidates (i.e., candidates who ran for election on multiple occasions) in a similar manner. The findings are consistent; the only exception is that when 90% and more of the repeat candidates are dropped, RDD-IK drops below the significance level (Online Appendix Figure A.2). 6.2. Checks for Model Assumptions Regarding the DID, the Online Appendix reports tests for parallel trends (Angrist and Pischke 2009). We interactModwith time dummies for the chronological distance from the election. None of the pre-election interactions is significant, and thus, there is no indication that the parallel trends assumption is violated (Online Appendix Table A.11). After the election, we observe significant interactions, which confirms the main results. To further ensure that the estimate is not an artifact of pre-existing differences between winners and nonwinners, we implement a placebo test (Bertrand et al. 2004). The test seeks to assess whether the effect arises in data where the null is known to hold. We follow prior work (Brynjolfsson et al. 2019,Shin et al. 2023,Chan et al. 2024) and restrict the estimation to the pre-election period and then create an artificial treatment event one year before the actual election (i.e., we move the treatment to the middle of the pre-period). We find no statistically significant effect of the placebo event, which corroborates the assumptions (Online Appendix Table A.12). In summary, we find no evidence that the parallel trends assumption is violated. Regarding the Cox model, we assess the proportional hazards assumption (Cox 1972,Lancaster 1990), that is, that the hazard ratio remains constant over time.Figure 5(a), provides the standard log-log plot for the full sample of candidates. The curves are reasonably parallel, which supports the proportional hazards assumption. We implement a test of the relationship between scaled Schoenfeld residuals and time for all subsamples (Grambsch and Therneau 1994). None of the tests indicates a violation of the assumption. For the RDD, we test the no sorting assumption, meaning that candidates are not able to self-select or self-sort into winning the election, for example, by manipulating the election outcome. We implement the standard test in its conventional and bias-corrected version (McCrary 2008,Calonico et al. 2014,Cattaneo et al. 2018). The test seeks to reject the null of clumping in the kernel density on either side of the vote share threshold. The tests do not lend evidence to a violation of the assumption (pconventional= 0.41,probust= 0.52).Figure 5(b), plots the kernel density distribution of the vote share margins and 90% confidence intervals. There is no evidence of a discontinuity at the cutoff, which supports the assumption. 6.3. Alienation of Nonwinners The positive effects we observe could be due to negative responses of those who fail to win an election. Were this to happen, it would violate the stable unit treatment value assumption, or SUTVA (Cox 1958, p. 19), and invalidate the use of nonwinners as comparison benchmark. To assess, we use the panel data samples (i.e, DID-CEM, DID-PSM, DID-All) restricted to non-winners and estimate a simple pre-post regression model of job changes, also including a control for a general time trend. The resulting coefficients are insignificant, which refutes concerns over alienation (Online Appendix Table A.13). 6.4. Validity of Applying the RDD in an STV Setting One could be concerned that the STV’s round-by-round reallocation of votes artificially depresses voter preferences, as indicated by the first preferences. If so, the distances between the most-voted nonwinner and least-voted winner might appear smaller than the disparity in voters’ true preferences. This should not be concerning for several reasons. First, although the STV procedure is not without criticism, we are not aware of any such bias being reported in prior studies (Hill 1988,Farrell 2011). Instead, the STV procedure helps to eliminate the hopeless nonwinners, and identifies clear winners, up front. The STV procedure then reallocates the remaining candidates to seats depending on expressed voter preferences while eliminating those candidates with relatively less preference from voters. Online Appendix Figure A.3 corroborates this fact in our data: comparing the distribution of first preferences, we see that the STV procedure eliminates the hopeless nonwinners at the left tail of the first-preference distribution as well as the clear winners at the right tail. Further corroborating is that our regression results hold when matching candidates based solely on their share of first preferences (Online Appendix Tables A.14 and A.15). 6.5. Sampling Bias Our study design depends on the assumption that the availability of LinkedIn profile data is not systematically associated with whether an individual wins or loses an election. Furthermore, it assumes that censoring in a candidate’s job change time series must be independent of treatment assignment (see, e.g.,Bor et al. 2014). One concern could be that the election outcome influences whether a candidate opens a LinkedIn account. Refuting this concern, we find that there is no statistically significant difference between winners and nonwinners on LinkedIn data availability, also not when conditioning on election year or community theme (Online Appendix Table A.16). A related concern is that the election outcome encourages candidates to put more effort into self-presentation on LinkedIn. Our data do not indicate when a profile was updated, such that we cannot directly test whether winning an election is associated with more information being reported in the profiles. An ex post comparison of winners’ and nonwinners’ LinkedIn profiles along various characteristics, including profile description length, endorsements received, and accomplishments, does not indicate any statistically significant difference (Online Appendix Tables A.17 and A.18).11Thus, at least from an ex post angle, winners and nonwinners do not differ along several pieces of nonjob-change-related information, which provides some confidence that the findings are not driven by changes in reporting behaviors. 6.6. Employment Gaps It is important to consider job changes with and without employment gaps because job changes with employment gaps could indicate a period of unemployment (i.e., a career harm rather than benefit). For our job change measure, we had chosen to retain jobs with employment gaps because such gaps are common and may be driven by a variety of factors, such as taking time off to raise a family, pursuing education or training, traveling, or taking a break from work for personal or professional reasons; that is, they will frequently not be due to layoffs. Nevertheless, we explore the stability of our results to an adjusted measure that excludes job changes with employment gaps. The goal of this exercise is to ensure that those job changes are not driving our findings.12The key challenge for such a test is that discarding observations artificially reduces variation in the outcome, so any loss of significance might be due to a decline in statistical power. Therefore, we proceed stepwise, as we did inSection 6.1; we begin by recoding 10% of the job changes with employment gaps (chosen at random) as 0 s (i.e., as no changes) and plot the resulting estimate, then 20%, and so on. Figure 6, (a) and (b), report the estimates forEquations (1)and (2). The estimates from the DID-CEM, DID-PSM, and RDD-5% estimation remain significant even when we recode all job changes with employment gaps. For DID-All and RDD-IK, the estimates do not lose significance until we recode 70% and more of the relevant job changes. The RDD-None estimates are significant until we recode 90% and more.Figure 6, (c) and (d), report a complementary test in which we recode the equivalent number of job changes without employment gap at random. For example, if candidate A experienced a job change with an employment gap, we recode another randomly selected candidate B’s job change—which did not show an employment gap—a s 0. Here, the RDD estimates turn insignificant when 100% is recoded. These observations support that job changes with employment gaps are not fundamentally driving the results; rather, omitting them is likely leading to a loss of statistical power.13
article.7. Mechanism Exploration: Admittedly, identifying the causal mechanisms that underlie the treatment effect poses a greater challenge than estimating the treatment effect itself. In the following, we present exploratory results, which are at best suggestive of the mechanisms at work. 7.1. Human and Social Capital To explore the human and social capital mechanisms, we use the indirect effects estimation ofBuis (2010),Hicks and Tingley (2011), andImai et al. (2011). The analysis seeks to isolate the portion of the causal effect of moderatorship on job changes that is attributable to improvements in human or social capital. This requires the estimation of two equations: The mediation test depends on the assumption of sequential ignorability, meaning thatMis independent conditional onMod. In nonlinear models, the standard difference of coefficients methods for estimating the average causal mediation effect (ACME) will no longer provide a consistent estimate under sequential ignorability (VanderWeele 2009,Imai et al. 2011). For these reasons, we use the procedure ofHicks and Tingley (2011), who extended the indirect effects approach to nonlinear models. This procedure first fits the models for outcome and mediator variables, then simulates the model parameters from their sampling distribution, then afterward simulates potential values for the outcome and the mediator for each draw of model parameters, and then computes the statistics of interest. Online Appendix B provides the technical details. Table 9shows the results. We cannot confirm a mediation effect for human capital; none of the models supports a significant effect ofModonTag Badges(i.e., columns 1, 3, and 5). We observe evidence for the social capital mechanism. All three conditions for mediation are met;Modhas a positive effect onChat contacts(i.e., columns 7, 9, and 11) yet is not significant for RDD-5%; there is a positive association betweenChat contactsandJob change(i.e., columns 8, 10, and 12); and the NIE is positive.15 Table 9.Mediation Tests for Human and Social Capital Table 9.Mediation Tests for Human and Social Capital Notes.Logit coefficients reported as odds ratios.Ngives the effective sample size in candidates. Regarding the sensitivity, Online Appendix Figure B.2 plots the results of theρ-sensitivity analysis (Hicks and Tingley 2011). The analysis quantifies the degree of sensitivity by looking at how large the parameterρ, the correlation between the error terms, must be for the ACME to be insignificant. Generally, the largerρ, the more robust the results are toward unobserved confounders. The results show that, to conclude that the true ACME for RDD-IK is not statistically different from 0, we must assume that an unobserved confounder that affects both social capital and job change in the same direction and makes the correlation between the error terms greater than 0.30. There are no objective standards for assessing the absolute values ofρ, but it indicates a relative level of robustness specific to the empirical setting and model, thus necessitating cross-study comparison (Keele et al. 2015). We are not aware of a study directly comparable to ours; however,Dimitriadis and Koning (2022)studied the effects of a social skills training program on entrepreneurs’ profits (mediated by social interactions among them) and reportedρ= 0.16.Carpena and Zia (2020)reported the findings of a field experiment on the returns to financial education and considered aρ= 0.23 as supportive of the mediation. Ourρis larger. Furthermore, we can consider the importance of the unobserved confounder in explaining the observed variation. For RDD-IK ACME to be 0, for example, an omitted confounder must explain 30% of the remaining variance in the mediator and 30% of the remaining variance in the outcome (i.e.,𝑅2𝑀×𝑅2𝑌=0.09RM2×RY2=0.09must be satisfied). There is also anecdotal evidence for the social capital mechanism. On Stack Exchange, we found several posts of moderators in which they described the social component of their role. Part of this involves interacting and bonding with other moderators, for example, “A good amount of my day is spent hanging out in the private chatroom for all of the Stack Exchange moderators. We talk to other moderators about moderation issues […].” Additionally, moderators mingle with power users, that is, users who are strongly engaged and support moderators. There are chatrooms on Stack Exchange in which power users of a community can exchange information with each other and the moderators. 7.2. Signaling To explore signaling, we implemented the test formally modeled byGraetz (2017,2021). It is based on the idea that signaling effects should deteriorate over time because of employers’ learning about the true quality of a candidate. If signaling is at play, we should observe that career benefits manifest relatively soon after the election. By contrast, human capital and social capital take time to accumulate, and thus effects resulting from these mechanisms should manifest after some time in the position. Therefore, we estimate the RDD separately for each half-year after the election, as reported inTable 10. We observe significant effects intandt+ 2. Interpreting these results via the model proposed byGraetz (2017,2021), the immediacy of the observed effect intis consistent with a signaling mechanism. article.8. Discussion: Our study makes two main contributions. First, we contribute to the debate on career benefits from participating in online communities (Lerner and Tirole 2002,Roberts et al. 2006,Hann et al. 2013). Departing fromLerner and Tirole (2002), several studies have suggested that community participation may be driven by an expectation that it leads to employment benefits (Hann et al. 2013,Huang and Zhang 2016,Xu et al. 2020). Causal evidence of community engagement leading to career benefits has so far been lacking. Our study provides such evidence, particularly focusing on community leadership. Demonstrating that contributors’ expectations are well-founded is important because, were this not the case, the resultant failure to manage contributor expectations would likely have undesirable long-run consequences for community commitment. Had we found little evidence of career benefits the natural implication would be for the platform operator to highlight other benefits of participation when attracting and engaging with members, for example, community or a sense of belonging. Given that we have confirmed the validity of career benefits, the implication is that operators of communities should indeed highlight those benefits to members. Second, we also contribute to research on the motivations behind private contributions to online communities (see, e.g.,Wasko and Faraj 2005,von Krogh et al. 2012,Wang et al. 2022). The notion that economic motives drive contributions has long been advocated, and relatively strong evidence exists that the perception of career benefits drives individuals’ contributions. However, our study presents the first rigorous empirical evidence that this perception is a reality. Finally, our study provides exploratory empirical evidence about possible mechanisms underlying resulting career benefits. That we find support for a social capital explanation complements prior explanations of human capital benefits as a driver, although we also, notably, find no evidence in support of the latter. What remains unclear, however, is the extent to which social capital benefits are perceived by contributors, especially compared with signaling benefits or human capital development. Understanding contributor expectations about the role of specific mechanisms is important. If individuals purposefully engage in communities with incorrect expectations about their signaling or the degree to which that engagement will result in the acquisition of market-relevant skills, then they are likely to engage in a suboptimal manner, selecting into thewrongactivities, for example. Future work can look to address this aspect. At the same time, understanding participant perceptions about specific mechanisms behind possible career benefits is also important for the design and management of online communities. For example, if community participants are not aware of the important role of social capital development as an underlying mechanism for career benefits, then they may not be sufficiently engaged in forming new social ties or engaging with new members, etc. Conversely, if participants believe that social capital benefits are a useful return to their engagement, then policies and mechanisms employed within communities are best designed with a focus on facilitating social engagement, and less so on confirming expertise, or on skill acquisition. It would also be valuable to assess the external validity of our findings with respect to other types of communities, for example, those focused on open-source software or online encyclopedias. Notably, Wikipedia also holds elections for leadership positions, which could be exploited to apply a similar econometric framework. One limitation of our research is that we cannot perfectly observe career benefits but have to rely on job changes. Another limitation relates to the matching of the LinkedIn profiles. Although our checks did not indicate a sampling bias, future work might consider using alternative data sources to elicit careers, for example, surveys and interviews, as inHann et al. (2013). Last, it is generally challenging to measure human and social capital and particularly so in the case of observational data from online sources. Our measures provided the best feasible option. However, future research should consider implementing more robust measures. For human capital, researchers could obtain data on test outcomes (e.g., university exams, ratings of managers, assessment center tests) or schooling (e.g., training hours, certificates). For social capital, researchers could obtain data on actual interactions (e.g., frequency of exchange between individuals, number of meetings attended together). Although we find support for social capital mechanisms using incoming chat contacts, we are unable to examine a measure of reciprocal chat contacts because there is too little variation in the measure. Moreover, our understanding of social capital would be improved here if we could assess which contacts in particular were driving the effects (e.g., power users, other moderators). Regarding the signaling mechanism, we observe some support, but we also observe that the results for later time periods are not consistently significant.
